hero.badge: AI POWERED · TRAINED ON INDIAN SPECIES
hero.subtitle: AI-powered snake detection for India. Upload a photo and know in seconds if you're safe.

facts.fact1: King Cobras are the only snakes in the world that build nests for their eggs.
facts.fact2: Some snakes can survive for months without a single meal by drastically lowering their metabolism.
facts.fact3: Snakes don't smell with their noses; they use their forked tongues to gather scent particles.
facts.fact4: Snakes do not have eyelids and cannot blink. They sleep with their eyes open!
facts.fact5: The Indian Rock Python is massive and can easily exceed 5 meters in length.
facts.fact6: The bite of a Common Krait is often completely painless, causing many victims to ignore it.
facts.fact7: Saw-scaled Vipers create a loud, sizzling warning sound by rubbing their specialized serrated scales together.
facts.fact8: Green Vine Snakes have horizontal, keyhole-shaped pupils giving them incredible binocular vision.
facts.fact9: Snakes are completely deaf to airborne sounds but are highly sensitive to vibrations in the ground.

emergency.title: Emergency Guide
emergency.subtitle: Snake Bite First Aid · Stay calm, act fast.
emergency.timerDesc: Time is critical. Antivenom is most effective within 1–2 hours.
emergency.do1: Stay calm — panic increases venom spread
emergency.do2: Immobilize the bitten limb below heart level
emergency.do3: Remove rings, watches, tight clothing immediately
emergency.do4: Note the snake's appearance (or photograph if safe)
emergency.do5: Get to the nearest government hospital fast
emergency.dont1: Cut the wound or try to suck out venom
emergency.dont2: Apply a tourniquet — it causes tissue death
emergency.dont3: Apply ice or any heat to the bite
emergency.dont5: Waste time on local remedies or healers
emergency.bigFourTitle: Snake-Specific First Aid · The Big Four
emergency.cobra_symptoms: Drooping eyelids, difficulty swallowing, paralysis, breathing trouble within 1-3 hours.
emergency.cobra_treatment: Polyvalent ASV. Maintain airway. Ventilator support may be needed.
emergency.krait_symptoms: Bites often PAINLESS — felt only during sleep. Abdominal pain, ptosis, paralysis.
emergency.krait_treatment: Critical: anyone waking with unexplained paralysis should be rushed to hospital with ASV.
emergency.russell_symptoms: Severe pain, rapid local swelling, bleeding from gums/urine, kidney failure.
emergency.russell_treatment: Polyvalent ASV, blood transfusion, dialysis may be required.
emergency.saw_symptoms: Swelling, bleeding disorders, blistering. Responsible for most snakebite deaths in India.
emergency.saw_treatment: Polyvalent ASV. Watch for systemic bleeding for 48-72 hours.
emergency.asvTitle: India uses Polyvalent Anti-Snake Venom (ASV) covering the Big Four
emergency.hospitalDesc: Snakebite victims must be rushed to a government hospital equipped with Anti-Snake Venom (ASV).

awareness.label: Learn
awareness.title: Snake Awareness
awareness.subtitle: Knowledge saves lives. Bust myths, understand seasonal risk, and learn how to live safely alongside snakes.
awareness.seasonDesc: Bite incidents peak during monsoon months when snakes are displaced from their shelters.
awareness.behavior1_desc: Sudden movement can trigger defensive behavior. Freeze and back away slowly.
awareness.behavior2_desc: Keep at least 2 metres away. Most snakes retreat if left alone.
awareness.behavior3_desc: Even injured or apparently dead snakes can bite. Never attempt handling.
awareness.behavior4_desc: Contact a trained rescuer rather than trying to kill or move the snake yourself.
awareness.tryAgain: Review the guide above and try again!
awareness.question: Question
awareness.fact1: Fact: Snakes are reptiles and do not naturally drink milk. They are carnivores and cannot properly digest dairy products. During festivals like Nag Panchami, snakes may drink milk only because they are dehydrated, not because they like it.
awareness.myth2: Snakes dance to a snake charmer's flute
awareness.fact2: Fact: Snakes do not hear airborne sounds the way humans do because they lack external ears. What appears to be 'dancing' is actually the snake following the movement of the flute and the charmer.
awareness.myth3: A snake remembers a person's face and takes revenge
awareness.fact3: Fact: Snakes do not have the cognitive ability to recognize and remember human faces for revenge. This is a widespread superstition with no scientific basis.
awareness.myth4: If one snake is killed, its mate will come looking for revenge
awareness.fact4: Fact: Snakes do not form lifelong partnerships or seek revenge. Another snake may be found nearby simply because both occupy the same habitat.
awareness.fact5: Fact: India has over 300 snake species, but only a small fraction are dangerously venomous. Most Indian snakes are non-venomous and harmless to humans.
awareness.myth6: Baby snakes are more dangerous than adult snakes
awareness.fact6: Fact: Baby venomous snakes can inject venom, but they are not necessarily more dangerous than adults. Adult snakes generally possess larger venom glands and can deliver more venom.
awareness.fact7: Fact: Snakes usually avoid humans. When a snake appears to chase someone, it is often trying to escape toward its shelter, which may happen to be in the same direction.
awareness.myth8: Applying a tourniquet or cutting a snakebite wound helps
awareness.fact8: Fact: These methods are dangerous and can worsen the injury. The correct response is to keep the victim calm, immobilize the affected limb, and seek immediate medical attention.
awareness.fact9: Fact: So-called 'snake stones' have no scientific ability to absorb or neutralize venom. Antivenom administered in a hospital is the only proven treatment for serious venomous snakebites.
awareness.fact10: Fact: The 'Nagmani' is a myth from folklore. No snake species possesses or produces any magical gem.
awareness.fact11: Fact: Snakes do not hypnotize prey. Animals may appear frozen due to fear or confusion, not because of any hypnotic power.
awareness.myth12: Touching a snake causes instant death
awareness.fact12: Fact: Most snakes are harmless. A bite from a non-venomous snake is usually not life-threatening, though it may still require medical care to prevent infection.
awareness.myth13: Snakes can sting with their tails
awareness.fact13: Fact: Indian snakes bite using their mouths. They do not have venomous stingers in their tails.
awareness.myth14: A snake crossing your path is a bad omen
awareness.fact14: Fact: This is a cultural belief and has no scientific basis. A snake crossing a path is simply an animal moving through its habitat.
awareness.fact15: Fact: Snakes help control rodent populations and play an important role in ecosystems. Unnecessary killing of snakes can harm ecological balance.
awareness.quiz1_q: Which snake bite is often painless and may be noticed only during sleep?
awareness.quiz2_q: What should you do first after a snake bite?
awareness.quiz2_o3: Stay calm and immobilize the limb
awareness.quiz3_q: How many snakes make up India's Big Four?
awareness.quiz4_q: Where is anti-snake venom commonly available free of cost?
awareness.quiz5_q: Which snake is non-venomous and often confused with a cobra?

rescuers.label: Local Heroes
rescuers.title: Find a Snake Rescuer Near You
rescuers.subtitle: Verified rescuers across India ready to safely relocate snakes from your area.
rescuers.story1_text: A 6-foot Rat Snake was rescued from a primary school in Pune and safely released into a nearby forest habitat, ensuring safety for both students and the snake.
rescuers.story2_text: A Spectacled Cobra found refuge in an office basement during heavy rains. A registered rescuer carefully bagged it without any injury and relocated it.
rescuers.story3_text: A homeowner spotted a highly venomous Common Krait near their porch. Thanks to SarpDrishti, a rescuer arrived in 15 minutes and safely removed the threat.
rescuers.story4_text: A bright green Vine Snake was found resting on a family's balcony plants. A rescuer gently guided it into a cloth bag and relocated it to a nearby wooded area.
rescuers.story5_text: Farmers in Maharashtra spotted a highly venomous Russell's Viper in a sugarcane field. They paused work and called a certified rescuer who safely removed it, preventing a potential tragedy.
rescuers.story6_text: A harmless Red Sand Boa was mistaken for a dangerous snake at a construction site. SarpDrishti helped identify it quickly, and a local expert safely moved it away from the machinery.
